unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: In this unit you learn about the world around us — the plants, the trees, the air, and the land and water we all share. You find out how to care for planet Earth by planting, watering, picking up litter and recycling. You learn the parts of a plant and why trees keep our air clean, and you practise saying thank you for the beautiful things in nature. By the end you can make your own promise to care for the Earth, just like Amal in this unit's story, “Amal and the Little Tree”.
unit.learningPath:
Watch the teacher lecture, then learn a few vocabulary words each day and say every word out loud.
Work through the six grammar practices: actions happening now, saying thank you for nature, and this, these, that and those.
Read “Mother Earth Day”, “Plants and Clean Air”, the Earth Day family text, the Earth poem and the story “Amal and the Little Tree”, then answer the twenty-three comprehension questions.
Complete the six speaking, six writing and six activity practices.
Join three live teacher sessions in each of the two weeks.
Finish with the ten-question checkpoint and the self-assessment.
unit.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 2 Unit 7 source pack under docs/lesson-authoring-super-prompt.md
unit.reviewStatus: Pilot rebuild - pending curriculum reviewer sign-off

visual.alt: Cartoon children planting seeds, watering young trees and sorting paper for recycling beside a river, with one plant labelled to show its roots, stem, leaves and flower.

vocabularyGroups.title: Words for Saying Thank You to Nature

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Putting seeds or young plants into the soil so they can grow.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Planting trees helps clean the air and gives us shade.
practiceSentences: Amal is planting a young tree beside the river.
Which month is best for planting these seeds?
“Come and help me with the planting,” said Adam.
I am planting three sunflowers in my own pot.
Planting a hedge gives small birds a safe home.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - l - a - n - t - i - n - g
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper beside you, tell the tutor what you would plant in your garden and ask it to check your planting sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Giving water to plants so that they do not dry out.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Nora is watering the flowers every morning before school.
practiceSentences: Adam is watering the seeds with a small can.
How often does watering help a young tree?
“Stop watering now, the soil is wet,” said Hana.
I help by watering our tomato plants after tea.
Watering the garden in the evening saves more water.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: w - a - t - e - r - i - n - g
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to sit with you, then describe how you water a plant and ask the tutor if your watering sentence is clear.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Lifting things off the ground and taking them away.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Picking up litter keeps our street clean and safe.
practiceSentences: Amal and Adam are picking up bags near the path.
Are you picking up the papers by the gate?
“Thank you for picking up that tin,” said Nora.
I am picking up leaves for my nature poster.
Picking up glass is a job for a grown-up.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - i - c - k - i - n - g, then u - p
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor, while your adult helper listens, what you would be picking up on a class litter walk and why.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Rubbish that people drop on the ground in public places.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Please do not drop litter; use a bin instead.
practiceSentences: Litter near the river can hurt fish and birds.
Who left all this litter beside the bus stop?
“There is litter under the swing,” said Theo.
I put my litter in the bin every playtime.
One bag of litter can take years to rot away.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - i - t - t - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult next to you, ask the tutor to name three places where litter causes trouble, then say which one surprised you.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Turning used things, like paper or glass, into something new.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Recycling paper helps save trees and keeps our air clean.
practiceSentences: Our school is recycling old newspapers into art paper.
Does recycling glass really save power at the factory?
“Recycling helps the Earth,” said Nora with a smile.
I am recycling my juice bottles in the green bin.
Recycling turns yesterday's rubbish into tomorrow's useful things.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - e - c - y - c - l - i - n - g
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Sit with your helper and ask the tutor to explain what happens to paper after recycling, then say the answer back in your own words.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The plant parts under the ground that drink up water.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The roots of the big tree go deep into the ground.
practiceSentences: Strong roots hold the little tree in the soil.
How far do the roots of a maize plant spread?
“Cover the roots gently,” said Hana to Amal.
I water the roots, not the leaves, of my plant.
Carrots are roots that we can wash and eat.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - o - o - t - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, ask the tutor why roots grow downwards, then explain the answer to your helper in one sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The main part of a plant that holds up the leaves.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Water travels up the stem to reach the leaves.
practiceSentences: A thin green stem pushed up through the soft soil.
Is the stem of a sunflower thick or thin?
“Do not bend the stem,” said Adam carefully.
I drew a long stem under my paper flower.
The stem carries food and water around the whole plant.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - t - e - m
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Show your helper a real stem, then ask the tutor to check the sentence you say about the job the stem does.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The flat green parts of a plant that make its food.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The green leaves make food for the plant using sunlight.
practiceSentences: Wide leaves gave Amal cool shade under the old tree.
Why do some leaves change colour in the dry season?
“Count the leaves on this branch,” said Nora.
I press flat leaves inside my nature book.
Leaves take in the dirty air and clean it for us.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - e - a - v - e - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Collect two leaves with an adult, describe how they differ, and ask the tutor which describing words you could add.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The colourful part of a plant that makes new seeds.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The garden is full of beautiful flowers this month.
practiceSentences: One small white flower opened on Amal's little tree.
Which flower in our garden smells the sweetest?
“Look, a bee is resting on that flower,” said Theo.
I gave my grandmother a yellow flower from our pot.
After the flower dies, tiny seeds are left behind.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - l - o - w - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper watching, ask the tutor what a flower turns into, then say the whole answer back as one sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Tiny plant parts that can grow into brand new plants.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We put the seeds in the soil and watered them.
practiceSentences: Hana gave Amal a handful of small brown seeds.
How many seeds are hiding inside this pawpaw?
“Plant the seeds in a row,” said Adam.
I keep my sunflower seeds in a paper bag.
Wind and birds carry seeds to faraway ground.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - e - e - d - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to open a fruit with you, count the seeds together, and ask the tutor to help you say the number in a full sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A tall plant with a wooden trunk, branches and leaves.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A bird built its nest high in the tall tree.
practiceSentences: The old tree by the house gives cool shade.
How long does a mango tree take to grow?
“This tree was a seed once,” said Hana.
I measure my little tree with a piece of string.
A tree holds water in the soil and stops it drying out.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - r - e - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Stand with your adult helper near a tree, describe it out loud, and ask the tutor for one more word to describe its bark.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The invisible gases all around us that we breathe in.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Plants help clean the air we breathe every day.
practiceSentences: After the rain, the air smelled fresh and clean.
Why does the air feel cooler under a big tree?
“Take a deep breath of this air,” said Nora.
I love the air near the river in the morning.
Smoke from burning rubbish makes the air dirty.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - i - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask the tutor to explain how leaves clean the air, then retell it to your helper in two sentences.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The clear liquid that people, animals and plants all need.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We must keep the river water clean for everyone.
practiceSentences: Theo carried water to the little trees each day.
How much water does one young tree need?
“Do not waste water,” said Adam as he turned the tap off.
I drink water after I play outside in the sun.
Clean water helps every living thing to stay strong.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: w - a - t - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask your helper to time you turning the tap off quickly, then ask the tutor to suggest one more way to save water.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The brown earth on the ground where plants grow.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The seeds grew well in the soft, dark soil.
practiceSentences: Amal dug a small hole in the soft soil.
Is this soil too dry for our new seeds?
“Press the soil down gently,” said Hana.
I washed the soil off my hands after gardening.
Good soil holds water so that roots can drink.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - o - i - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Feel some soil with an adult, describe it in three words, and ask the tutor whether your describing words fit.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The planet that we all live on; it is our home.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We should all take care of planet Earth.
practiceSentences: The Earth gives us water, soil and clean air.
What can one child do to help the Earth?
“The Earth is saying thank you,” said Hana.
I made a poster of the Earth for our wall.
Every day can be a good day for the Earth.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: E - a - r - t - h
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your adult helper listening, ask the tutor to name one way children help the Earth, then say whether you already do it.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: Words for Saying Thank You to Nature
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Feeling happy and pleased about something good that has happened.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I am glad that our village looks beautiful again.
practiceSentences: Amal was glad to see birds back in the branches.
Are you glad that the rain has come at last?
“I am glad you came to help,” said Nora.
I am glad that my little tree has a flower.
We felt glad when the path was clean and tidy.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: g - l - a - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Sit with an adult and tell the tutor one thing in nature you are glad about, then ask it to check your sentence.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: Words for Saying Thank You to Nature
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Feeling good inside, so that you want to smile.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Adam felt happy when the first green stem appeared.
practiceSentences: Clean, green places make our whole village happy.
What makes you happy about the world around you?
“I am happy that trees give us fresh air,” said Theo.
I am happy when I hear birds sing at sunrise.
Hana looked happy as she watched the young trees.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - a - p - p - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask your helper to listen while you tell the tutor what makes you happy outdoors, then ask for one more feeling word.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: Words for Saying Thank You to Nature
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Feeling glad for something good and wanting to say thank you.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I am thankful for clean water and fresh air.
practiceSentences: Amal felt thankful for the shade of the old tree.
What are you thankful for in nature today?
“We are thankful for the rain,” said the villagers.
I am thankful for the soil that feeds our food.
Being thankful helps us look after what we have.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - h - a - n - k - f - u - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, say three thankful sentences to the tutor and ask which one sounded most natural.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: Words for Saying Thank You to Nature
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To know that something is good and to be glad about it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I appreciate the beautiful flowers in our school garden.
practiceSentences: Nora and Theo appreciate the cool shade at midday.
Do you appreciate the fresh air after heavy rain?
“I appreciate your help today,” said Hana to Amal.
I appreciate clean water because every living thing needs it.
We appreciate the tall trees that hold the soil together.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - p - p - r - e - c - i - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor, with your helper present, to check that your appreciate sentence has no extra word after appreciate.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: Words for Saying Thank You to Nature
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Mattering a lot, so that we must care about it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: It is important to take care of our planet.
practiceSentences: Trees are important because they clean the air we breathe.
Why is clean water so important for every family?
“This is important work,” said Adam, lifting the box.
I think it is important to put litter in the bin.
Roots are important, even though we cannot see them.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: i - m - p - o - r - t - a - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult in the room, tell the tutor why one nature word from this unit is important, then ask for feedback.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name and describe ways to care for the environment, including planting, watering, picking up litter and recycling.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Five correct answers in Activity 1, the eight-item sort in Activity 5, and four action sentences recorded in Speaking 1.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name the parts of a plant and say what each part does.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four correct matches in Activity 2, a fully labelled plant drawing in Writing 3, and every part named aloud in Speaking 2.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Explain why plants and trees are important for clean air.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Correct answers to comprehension questions 6 to 9 on “Plants and Clean Air”, and a six-sentence report under three subheadings in Writing 4.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Say and write what someone is doing right now, using am, is or are with a verb ending in -ing.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Five correct helper words in Grammar 1, five correct -ing spellings in Activity 4, and five report sentences in Grammar 4.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Show appreciation for nature using phrases such as “I’m glad that…” and “I appreciate…”.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four completed phrase sentences in Grammar 2, a four-line thank-you card in Grammar 5, and one full round of the Speaking 6 thank-you circle.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use this and these, that and those to point at things that are near and far.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four correct choices in Grammar 3, eight garden-tour sentences in Grammar 6, and four walk sentences in Activity 6.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Write simple sentences and a short thank-you letter to the Earth.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Ten sentences in Writing 1, the five-part letter in Writing 2, and correct capital letters and full stops throughout both pieces.

readings.title: Mother Earth Day
readings.passageScript:
Read this short text:
Mother Earth Day is a special day when people all around the world show they care about our planet. On this day, people do many good things. Some people plant trees and flowers, because trees and plants help clean the air we breathe. Some people pick up litter from parks, beaches and streets, to keep the Earth clean and safe for animals. Other people learn about recycling, which helps save trees and water. Every day can be Earth Day if we remember to care for our planet!

readings.title: Plants and Clean Air
readings.passageScript:
Read this short text:
Plants are very important for our planet. The green leaves of trees and plants clean the dirty air. They take in a gas called carbon dioxide and give out oxygen. Oxygen is what we need to breathe. This is why big forests are called the “lungs of the Earth” — they help the Earth breathe! Plants can clean the air inside our homes and schools too, and they make our spaces more beautiful. Taking care of plants is a way of taking care of ourselves.

readings.title: A Family on Mother Earth Day
readings.passageScript:
Listen to this text about one family. Ask an adult to read it aloud, then read it again by yourself.
“Today is Mother Earth Day. Sami and his family are doing different things to help the environment. Sami’s father is planting trees with his uncle. His mother is counting birds with his aunt. Sami’s brother is picking up litter with his friends. Sami is making paper flowers with his grandmother from old newspapers. The family promise to do something good for the Earth every day, not only on Mother Earth Day.”

readings.title: A Poem for the Earth
readings.passageScript:
Listen, then read along:
I’m glad the sky is painted blue,
And Earth is painted green,
With such a lot of nice fresh air
All sandwiched in between.

readings.title: Amal and the Little Tree
readings.genre: A gentle nature story with dialogue.
readings.theme: Caring for the Earth — planting, watering, and keeping our places clean.
readings.setting: A small village beside a river that has grown dry.
readings.passageScript:
Amal lived in a small village beside a river. She loved to sit under the old tree by her house. Its green leaves gave her cool shade, and birds sang in its branches every morning.
One hot day, Amal saw litter all along the path to the river. Old bags and papers lay in the soil.
“Look at all this litter,” said Amal to her brother Adam. “Our village is not clean.”
“You are right,” said Adam. “Let us pick it up.”
Amal and Adam spent the morning picking up the litter. They put the paper in a box for recycling. Their friend Nora came to help too.
“When we recycle this paper,” said Nora, “fewer trees are cut down. Recycling helps the Earth.”
Soon the path was clean. Amal felt happy. “I’m glad that our village looks beautiful again,” she said.
But Amal looked sad when she saw the river. It was almost dry. Along the bank, where tall trees once grew, the land was empty and brown.
“Where did all the trees go?” asked Amal.
Her grandmother, Hana, was sitting nearby. “People cut them down for wood,” she said softly. “Without the trees, the sun dried the earth, and the river grew small. Trees clean the air and hold the water in the soil.”
“Then we must plant new trees!” said Amal.
Grandmother Hana smiled. She gave Amal a handful of small seeds. “Here,” she said. “Little things can make a big difference.”
The next morning, Amal and Adam dug small holes in the soft soil. Theo, the boy from next door, ran over to watch.
“What are you doing?” asked Theo.
“We are planting seeds,” said Amal. “These seeds will grow into tall trees one day.”
“Can I help?” asked Theo.
“Yes! You can be my partner,” said Amal, laughing.
Every morning, Amal was watering the little seeds. She watched the soil carefully. One day, a tiny green stem pushed up from the ground. Then came two small leaves.
“Look, Adam!” cried Amal. “The roots are growing under the ground, and the stem is growing up to the sky!”
The children looked after their plants all through the dry season. Theo carried water. Nora pulled out the weeds. Slowly, the little trees grew taller and stronger.
After many months, one small tree grew its very first flower. It was white and soft, and it smelled sweet.
“Our tree has a flower!” said Amal. “Soon there will be seeds, and we can plant even more trees.”
The whole village came to look. The children had planted a row of little trees along the river bank. Birds began to visit again. The air smelled fresh and clean.
Grandmother Hana put her hand on Amal’s shoulder. “You cared for the Earth,” she said, “and the Earth is saying thank you.”
That night, Amal looked at the young trees under the moon. “I’m thankful for the trees, the water and the clean air,” she whispered. “I promise to take care of you always.”
And every day after that, Amal remembered her grandmother’s words: little things can make a big difference.

comprehension.question: What is Mother Earth Day?
comprehension.correctAnswer: It is a special day when people all around the world show that they care about our planet.
comprehension.explanation: The very first sentence of the text explains what the day is and who takes part in it.

comprehension.question: Name three things the text says people do on Mother Earth Day.
comprehension.correctAnswer: They plant trees and flowers, they pick up litter from parks, beaches and streets, and they learn about recycling.
comprehension.explanation: The middle of the text lists three different jobs, one in each sentence.

comprehension.question: The text gives one reason for planting trees and flowers. What is it?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Trees and plants help clean the air we breathe.
comprehension.explanation: The word because in the third sentence points straight to the reason.

comprehension.question: Why does the text say picking up litter matters?
comprehension.correctAnswer: It keeps the Earth clean and safe for animals.
comprehension.explanation: The sentence about parks, beaches and streets ends with the reason for the work.

comprehension.question: Can Earth Day be only one day a year? Use the last sentence to explain your answer.
comprehension.correctAnswer: No. The last sentence says that every day can be Earth Day if we remember to care for our planet.
comprehension.explanation: This question asks you to read the closing sentence and turn it into your own answer.

comprehension.question: Why does the text say plants are very important for our planet?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Their green leaves clean the dirty air, so the air we breathe stays good.
comprehension.explanation: The second sentence names the job that green leaves do for the whole planet.

comprehension.question: What gas do plants take in, and what gas do they give out?
comprehension.correctAnswer: They take in carbon dioxide and give out oxygen.
comprehension.explanation: One sentence names both gases, and the next sentence explains why oxygen matters to us.

comprehension.question: Why does the text call big forests the “lungs of the Earth”?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because they help the Earth breathe, in the way that our lungs help us breathe.
comprehension.explanation: The words after the dash explain the picture that the nickname paints.

comprehension.question: The text says that taking care of plants is taking care of ourselves. Explain why that is true.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Plants give out the oxygen we need and clean the air in our homes and schools, so looking after them keeps us well.
comprehension.explanation: You have to join two ideas from the text: what plants give out, and where they clean the air.

comprehension.question: What is Sami’s father doing, and who is helping him?
comprehension.correctAnswer: He is planting trees, and his uncle is helping him.
comprehension.explanation: The second sentence of the family text names both the action and the helper.

comprehension.question: What is Sami making, and what is he using to make it?
comprehension.correctAnswer: He is making paper flowers, and he is using old newspapers.
comprehension.explanation: Listen for the sentence about Sami and his grandmother near the end of the text.

comprehension.question: What does the family promise to do?
comprehension.correctAnswer: They promise to do something good for the Earth every day, not only on Mother Earth Day.
comprehension.explanation: The last sentence of the text holds the family promise.

comprehension.question: Which two colours does the poem name, and what is each colour for?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Blue is for the sky and green is for the Earth.
comprehension.explanation: The first two lines of the poem pair one colour with one thing each.

comprehension.question: What does the poet say is “sandwiched in between” the sky and the Earth?
comprehension.correctAnswer: A lot of nice fresh air.
comprehension.explanation: The last two lines describe the air as if it were the filling in a sandwich.

comprehension.question: What did Amal and Adam find all along the path to the river?
comprehension.correctAnswer: They found litter: old bags and papers lying in the soil.
comprehension.explanation: The second paragraph of the story describes exactly what Amal saw on the hot day.

comprehension.question: What did the children do with the paper they picked up, and why is that good for the Earth?
comprehension.correctAnswer: They put it in a box for recycling. Nora explains that when we recycle paper, fewer trees are cut down.
comprehension.explanation: Nora gives the reason in the line she speaks just after the litter is collected.

comprehension.question: Why was the river almost dry?
comprehension.correctAnswer: People had cut the trees down for wood, so the sun dried the earth and the river grew small.
comprehension.explanation: Grandmother Hana explains the chain of events in one short speech.

comprehension.question: What did Grandmother Hana give Amal, and what did she say about little things?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She gave her a handful of small seeds and said that little things can make a big difference.
comprehension.explanation: This moment starts the whole planting plan, and the same words return at the end of the story.

comprehension.question: Name the parts of the plant that Amal saw growing, starting with the roots.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Roots, then the stem, then the leaves, and many months later the flower.
comprehension.explanation: The story shows the parts in growing order, so read those paragraphs one after the other.

comprehension.question: How did the village change after the children planted the trees?
comprehension.correctAnswer: A row of little trees grew along the river bank, birds began to visit again, and the air smelled fresh and clean.
comprehension.explanation: The paragraph where the whole village comes to look lists three changes together.

comprehension.question: Grandmother Hana said, “Little things can make a big difference.” What little thing did the children do, and what big difference did it make?
comprehension.correctAnswer: They planted and watered a handful of small seeds. Those seeds grew into a row of trees that brought the birds back and made the air fresh and clean.
comprehension.explanation: Match the small action at the start of the story to the large change at the end.

comprehension.question: What is one small thing you could do this week to care for the world around you?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Personal answer. Accept any real and safe action, such as putting litter in a bin, watering a plant or recycling paper at home.
comprehension.explanation: A good answer names one action and says when the child will do it.

comprehension.question: How do you feel when a place is clean and green, and how do you feel when you see litter? Give a reason for each feeling.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Personal answer. Look for one feeling word for each place with a reason, for example glad because clean air is nicer to breathe, and sad because litter can hurt animals.
comprehension.explanation: Two feelings and two reasons are needed, so plan the answer out loud before writing it.

grammar.title: Present Continuous: Actions Happening Now
grammar.explanation: We use the present continuous to say what is happening right now. In this unit it lets you describe what people are doing to help the Earth, such as planting, watering or picking up litter.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use am, is or are together with a verb that ends in -ing.
Use am with I: “I am planting a tree.”
Use is with he, she and it: “She is watering the flowers.”
Use are with you, we and they: “They are picking up litter.”
grammar.commonMistake: Some children write “She watering the plants.” The little helping word has gone missing, so the sentence should read “She is watering the plants.”
grammar.memoryTip: Every sentence needs two parts: a helper (am, is or are) plus a verb ending in -ing. One without the other is not finished.
grammar.practice:
Choose am, is or are for each sentence.
1. I ____ planting seeds.
2. He ____ watering the garden.
3. The children ____ picking up litter.
4. We ____ recycling old paper.
5. She ____ counting the birds.

grammar.title: Present Continuous: Report the Planting Day
grammar.explanation: A reporter tells us what is happening while it happens. In this task you use am, is and are with -ing verbs to report a planting day exactly as it takes place.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Match the helping word to the person before you add the -ing verb.
I am digging a small hole in the soil.
Theo is carrying water to the young trees.
Nora and Adam are pulling out the weeds.
We are planting a row of trees by the river.
grammar.commonMistake: It is easy to use is for more than one person, as in “Nora and Adam is pulling out the weeds.” Two people need are, so write “Nora and Adam are pulling out the weeds.”
grammar.memoryTip: Count the people first. One person takes is, and two or more people take are.
grammar.practice: Look again at “Amal and the Little Tree”. Write five sentences that report the planting day as it happens. Use I am once, he is or she is twice, and they are twice.

grammar.title: Saying Thank You for Nature
grammar.explanation: We can use special phrases to show that we are glad about, or thankful for, the beautiful things in nature. These phrases let you share a feeling instead of only naming a thing.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start your sentence with one of these phrases, then say what you are thankful for.
“I’m glad that…” gives “I’m glad that the sky is blue.”
“I’m happy that…” gives “I’m happy that trees give us fresh air.”
“I appreciate…” gives “I appreciate the beautiful flowers.”
“I’m thankful for…” gives “I’m thankful for clean water.”
“It’s important to…” gives “It’s important to take care of our planet.”
grammar.commonMistake: Some children write “I appreciate for the flowers.” The word appreciate does not need for, so write “I appreciate the flowers.” We do say “I’m thankful for the flowers.”
grammar.memoryTip: Appreciate hugs the thing straight away, with no for in between. Thankful always brings a for along with it.
grammar.practice:
Finish each sentence with your own idea about nature.
1. I’m glad that ______.
2. I appreciate ______.
3. I’m thankful for ______.
4. It’s important to ______.

grammar.title: Saying Thank You: Write a Nature Thank-You Card
grammar.explanation: When you write a card, you choose a different phrase for each line so that the message never sounds the same twice. This task asks you to use four thank-you phrases in one short card.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Give every line its own phrase and its own nature idea.
I’m glad that the birds are singing again.
I’m happy that our path is clean.
I appreciate the shade of the old tree.
I’m thankful for the rain that fills the river.
grammar.commonMistake: Starting all four lines with “I’m glad that…” makes a card that says very little. Change the phrase in every line so that each one adds something new.
grammar.memoryTip: Four lines, four different openers. Tick each phrase off your list as soon as you have used it.
grammar.practice: Write a four-line thank-you card to nature. Use “I’m glad that…”, “I’m happy that…”, “I appreciate…” and “I’m thankful for…”, one in each line, and name a different thing from this unit every time.

grammar.title: this and these, that and those
grammar.explanation: We use these little words to point at things. In this unit you use them to point at plants, flowers and trees, whether they are close to you or further away.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
this points at one thing that is near: “This plant is a tomato plant.”
these points at two or more things that are near: “These plants are onions.”
that points at one thing that is far: “That tree is very tall.”
those points at two or more things that are far: “Those flowers are red.”
grammar.commonMistake: Some children write “This plants are onions.” When there is more than one thing and it is near, the word changes to these, so write “These plants are onions.”
grammar.memoryTip: Near words are this and these. Far words are that and those. Add the extra letters whenever there is more than one thing.
grammar.practice:
Choose the right word.
1. ____ (This / These) flower near my hand is yellow.
2. ____ (That / Those) trees far away are very tall.
3. ____ (This / These) seeds in my hand are very small.
4. ____ (That / Those) bird up in the sky is singing.

grammar.title: this, these, that and those: Garden Tour
grammar.explanation: On a tour you point at things and name them for your visitor. You must choose the right pointing word each time, thinking about how near the thing is and how many of them there are.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Ask two questions before you choose: is it near or far, and is it one or more than one?
Near and one: This seed is very small.
Near and many: These leaves feel smooth.
Far and one: That tree grows by the river.
Far and many: Those birds are building a nest.
grammar.commonMistake: Children sometimes use that for something they are holding, as in “That seed in my hand is small.” If you can touch it, it is near, so write “This seed in my hand is small.”
grammar.memoryTip: Touch test first, counting test second. Can you touch it? Use this or these. Is there more than one? Add the extra letters.
grammar.practice: Take a tour of a garden, a park or a window box with an adult. Write eight sentences about what you see: two with this, two with these, two with that and two with those. Say each sentence aloud as you point.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Talk About Environmental Actions
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Look at four actions that help the Earth: planting trees, watering plants, picking up litter and recycling.
For each one say “This person is ______.” using am, is or are with an -ing word.
Then add “______ helps the environment because ______.”
Finish with “I can ______ to help the Earth.” Record all four sets and listen back for clear -ing endings.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, say your four action sentences to the tutor and ask it to check that every helping word matches its -ing verb.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Name the Parts of a Plant
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Stand beside a real plant, or use a labelled picture, while an adult watches.
Point to each part and say “These are the ______.” for the roots and the leaves, and “This is the ______.” for the stem and the flower.
Then say “The ______ help the plant to ______.” for two of the parts.
Finish with “Without ______, the plant cannot ______.” for two more parts.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask your helper to hold up the plant while you tell the tutor what each part does, then ask the tutor which part you explained most clearly.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — Say Thank You to Nature
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Think about trees, flowers, clean air, water, animals and the sky.
Say one sentence about each of them, beginning “I’m glad that ______.”
Then say “I appreciate ______ because ______.” about three of them.
Finish with “It’s important to protect ______ because ______.” Remember that appreciate never takes the word for.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Sit with an adult and say your thank-you sentences to the tutor, then ask it whether you used appreciate without the word for.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Plant Guide
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Be the guide for a two-minute plant tour at home, in a park or at school, with an adult with you.
Name four things you can see and point at each one, using this, these, that and those at least once each.
Give one fact about every thing you name, such as “These leaves make food for the plant.”
Record your tour and check that each pointing word matched how near the thing really was.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Play your plant tour to the tutor while your adult helper listens, and ask it to tell you which pointing word you used best.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Earth-Care Reporter
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Report on one thing your family or your class is doing to care for the Earth.
Start with “Today we are ______.” and say who is helping.
Give one reason using because, such as “because trees clean the air we breathe.”
End with a promise: “Tomorrow I am going to ______.” Record the report and play it to your family.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor, with an adult next to you, to listen to your report and to name every because reason that it hears.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — Thank-You Circle
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Sit in a circle with your family or your class for the unit showcase.
Each person says one sentence that begins “I’m thankful for ______.” about something in nature.
Listen carefully, then repeat the person before you, like this: “Nora is thankful for the rain.”
Record one full round and count how many different nature words the circle used.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor three of the thank-you sentences from your circle, with an adult listening, and ask it to suggest one more nature word your group could add.

writing.title: Writing 1 — Environmental Actions
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write five sentences about what people are doing to help the Earth. Use am, is or are with a verb ending in -ing in every sentence. After each one, add a second sentence that says why the action is good.
writing.modelText:
The boy is planting a tree. Trees give us clean air to breathe.
The girl is watering the flowers. Plants need water to grow.
Adam is picking up litter by the river. Litter can hurt fish and birds.
writing.successCriteria: Every action sentence has a helping word and an -ing verb, every reason sentence names one benefit, and all ten sentences start with a capital letter and end with a full stop.
writing.support: Choose your actions from Group 1 of the word list: planting, watering, picking up and recycling.
writing.extension: Join one action and its reason into a single sentence using because.

writing.title: Writing 2 — A Thank-You Letter to the Earth
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a short letter to the Earth. Plan it in five steps: a greeting, thank you for two special things, why those things matter, a promise to help, and a closing with your name.
writing.modelText:
Dear Earth,
Thank you for the tall trees that give us clean air. I appreciate the clean water that helps every living thing.
Without your soil, no seed could grow into food.
I promise to pick up litter and to plant a tree this year.
Your friend, Amal
writing.successCriteria: My letter has a greeting, two thank-you sentences, one sentence that says why, one promise and a closing with my name.
writing.support: Use two phrases from Grammar 2, such as “I appreciate…” and “I’m thankful for…”.
writing.extension: Draw the two things you thanked the Earth for, and label each drawing.

writing.title: Writing 3 — Story Response: Amal and the Little Tree
writing.promptAndInstructions: Answer three tasks about “Amal and the Little Tree”. First draw Amal’s little tree and label the roots, the stem, the leaves and the flower. Next write one sentence about the job each labelled part does. Finish with one sentence that thanks nature, beginning “I’m thankful for ______.”
writing.modelText:
The roots drink water from the soil.
The stem holds the leaves and the flower up high.
The leaves make food for the tree using sunlight.
The flower will make new seeds.
I’m thankful for the shade of the old tree.
writing.successCriteria: My drawing has four correct labels, each part has one sentence about its job, and my thank-you sentence names something real from nature.
writing.support: Look again at the paragraph where the stem and the two small leaves first appear in the story.
writing.extension: Add a caption under your drawing that says how long the little tree took to grow its first flower.

writing.title: Writing 4 — How a Plant Helps
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a short report of six sentences that explains how plants help our planet. Use three subheadings: What plants take in, What plants give out, and Why that matters to us. Put two sentences under each subheading.
writing.modelText:
What plants take in
Green leaves take in a gas called carbon dioxide.
The roots take in water from the soil.
What plants give out
Leaves give out oxygen, which is the gas we breathe.
Big forests give out so much oxygen that people call them the lungs of the Earth.
Why that matters to us
Clean air keeps our lungs healthy at home and at school.
Plants make our rooms and our streets more beautiful too.
writing.successCriteria: My report has three subheadings, two sentences under each one, and it uses the words carbon dioxide and oxygen correctly.
writing.support: Reread “Plants and Clean Air” and underline the sentence that names both gases.
writing.extension: Add a labelled diagram of one leaf that shows the gas going in and the gas coming out.

writing.title: Writing 5 — My Earth-Care Plan
writing.promptAndInstructions: Plan one week of caring for the Earth. Write six numbered steps, one for each day from Monday to Saturday, and say who will help you with each step.
writing.modelText:
1. On Monday I will pick up litter in our yard with my sister.
2. On Tuesday I will water the young tree with Adam.
3. On Wednesday I will put paper in the recycling box with my mother.
4. On Thursday I will plant three seeds in a pot with Nora.
5. On Friday I will turn the tap off quickly to save water.
6. On Saturday I will teach my whole plan to my class.
writing.successCriteria: Each step is numbered, names a day, names one Earth-care action and says who is helping me.
writing.support: Choose your actions from the Word Wall: planting, watering, picking up and recycling.
writing.extension: Tick each step when you finish it, then write one sentence about the step you enjoyed most.

writing.title: Writing 6 — My Promise to the Earth Poster
writing.promptAndInstructions: Make a poster that shows your promise to the Earth. Give the poster a title, write three promise sentences that begin “I promise to ______.”, and add one thank-you line that begins “I appreciate ______.” Draw one picture for each promise.
writing.modelText:
My Promise to the Earth
I promise to put every piece of litter in a bin.
I promise to water our little tree each morning.
I promise to recycle our old newspapers.
I appreciate the fresh air that the trees give us.
writing.expectedLength: A title plus 4 sentences and 3 drawings
writing.successCriteria: My poster has a clear title, three different promises, one appreciate line with no for after appreciate, and one picture for every promise.
writing.support: Say each promise out loud before you write it, and check that it begins with a capital letter.
writing.extension: Ask two people at home to sign your poster and to add one promise of their own.

activities.title: Activity 1 — Fill in the Blanks (Caring for the Earth)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Fill each blank with one action word from this list: Planting, Watering, Picking up, Recycling, Using.
1. ______ trees helps clean the air we breathe.
2. ______ plants helps them grow strong and healthy.
3. ______ litter keeps our environment clean and safe for animals.
4. ______ paper, plastic and glass helps save resources.
5. ______ less water helps us have enough for the future.
activities.answerSummary: 1. Planting, 2. Watering, 3. Picking up, 4. Recycling, 5. Using.

activities.title: Activity 2 — Match the Plant Part to Its Job
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each plant part to the job it does, then write the four pairs out in full sentences.
Parts: roots, stem, leaves, flower
Jobs: (a) hold up the plant and carry water up to the leaves; (b) take in water from the soil; (c) make seeds for new plants; (d) make food for the plant using sunlight
activities.answerSummary: roots → b, stem → a, leaves → d, flower → c.

activities.title: Activity 3 — True or False (Nature Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False for each fact. If it is false, write the correct sentence underneath it.
1. Planting trees helps clean the air.
2. Roots grow above the ground.
3. Plants take in carbon dioxide and give out oxygen.
4. Plants do not need water to grow.
activities.answerSummary: 1. True. 2. False, roots grow below the ground. 3. True. 4. False, plants need water or they will die.

activities.title: Activity 4 — Earth Day Picture Report
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write the correct -ing form of the verb in brackets, then read the whole sentence aloud.
1. Sami’s father is ______ (plant) trees with his uncle.
2. His mother is ______ (count) the birds in the garden.
3. Sami’s brother is ______ (pick) up litter with his friends.
4. Sami is ______ (make) paper flowers from old newspapers.
5. We are ______ (recycle) our bottles and our tins.
activities.answerSummary: 1. planting, 2. counting, 3. picking, 4. making (drop the e), 5. recycling (drop the e).

activities.title: Activity 5 — Care or Harm Sort
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort these eight actions into two columns headed Cares for the Earth and Harms the Earth: planting a tree, dropping a bottle on the path, watering a young plant, leaving the tap running all day, recycling old paper, burning rubbish beside the road, picking up litter in the park, cutting down trees for wood.
activities.answerSummary: Cares for the Earth: planting a tree, watering a young plant, recycling old paper, picking up litter in the park. Harms the Earth: dropping a bottle on the path, leaving the tap running all day, burning rubbish beside the road, cutting down trees for wood.

activities.title: Activity 6 — This, That, These, Those Walk
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Take a short walk outside with an adult. Stop four times and write one sentence each time.
1. Point at one thing you can touch and begin with This.
2. Point at two or more things you can touch and begin with These.
3. Point at one thing far away and begin with That.
4. Point at two or more things far away and begin with Those.
activities.answerSummary: Four sentences, one for each pointing word. Check that this and these name things within reach, that that and those name things further away, and that the plural words are used only for more than one thing.

assignments.title: The World Around Us Earth-care portfolio
assignments.instructions: Put together three pieces of work. First finish “Writing 6 — My Promise to the Earth Poster” and read it aloud once. Next record “Speaking 5 — Earth-Care Reporter”, telling us what your family or your class is doing. Finally finish “Writing 4 — How a Plant Helps” with all three of its subheadings. Check the three pieces against the writing and speaking rubrics before you hand them in together.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: Which word means rubbish that people drop on the ground in public places?
quizzes.explanation: Litter is dropped rubbish. Sunshine is light from the sun, oxygen is a gas, and a branch is part of a tree.

quizzes.question: What does recycling do?
quizzes.options: It waters a plant so that it grows. | It counts the birds in a garden. | It measures how tall a tree is. | It turns used things into something new.
quizzes.correctAnswer: It turns used things into something new.
quizzes.explanation: Recycling makes used paper, glass or plastic into something new to use again. The other three describe completely different jobs.

quizzes.question: Which of these makes food for a plant using sunlight?
quizzes.options: The green leaves | The brown soil | The clean air | The falling rain
quizzes.explanation: Leaves use sunlight to make the plant's food. Soil, air and rain are around the plant, but they are not parts of it.

quizzes.question: Amal put the tiny ______ into the soil, and months later they grew into trees.
quizzes.explanation: Seeds are the tiny plant parts that grow into new plants. Buttons, shoes and roads never grow.

quizzes.question: Amal is holding two small seeds in her hand. Which sentence is correct?
quizzes.options: This seeds in my hand are small. | These seeds in my hand are small. | Those seeds in my hand are small. | That seeds in my hand are small.
quizzes.correctAnswer: These seeds in my hand are small.
quizzes.explanation: The seeds are near her hand and there is more than one of them, so the pointing word is these.

quizzes.question: Which of these actions keeps our environment clean and safe for animals?
quizzes.options: Dropping a bottle on the path | Leaving the tap running all day | Burning rubbish beside the road | Picking up litter in the park
quizzes.correctAnswer: Picking up litter in the park
quizzes.explanation: Picking up litter keeps parks and streets safe for animals. The other three choices leave harm behind them.

quizzes.question: What does the flower of a plant make?
quizzes.options: New seeds | Fresh soil | Cold rain | Bright sunlight
quizzes.explanation: A flower makes the seeds that grow into new plants. Soil, rain and sunlight are not made by any flower.

quizzes.question: According to “Plants and Clean Air”, which gas do plants give out?
quizzes.explanation: Plants take in carbon dioxide and give out oxygen, which is the gas that we breathe.

quizzes.question: Look at the picture: Nora ______ the flowers right now.
quizzes.options: watering | are watering | is watering | am watering
quizzes.explanation: Nora is one person, so she takes is, and the verb keeps its -ing ending.

quizzes.question: Which sentence uses the word appreciate correctly?
quizzes.options: I appreciate for the tall trees. | I appreciate the tall trees. | I am appreciate the tall trees. | I appreciate to the tall trees.
quizzes.explanation: Appreciate goes straight to the thing, with no for, no to and no extra helping word.

liveSessions.title: The World Around Us: Earth-care words and plant parts
liveSessions.beforeSession: Watch the teacher lecture and learn Group 1 of the word list: planting, watering, picking up, litter and recycling.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min welcome and word-wall warm-up; 10 min Group 1 action mimes; 10 min plant-part labelling on a large diagram; 15 min Speaking 2 in pairs; 5 min reflection
liveSessions.afterSession: Learn Group 2 of the word list and label your own plant drawing.

liveSessions.title: Shared reading: Amal and the Little Tree
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read “Amal and the Little Tree” once at home and underline every Unit 7 word that you meet.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min story recap; 15 min shared reading aloud with attention to speech marks; 15 min story questions 15 to 20; 10 min hot-seating Amal and Grandmother Hana
liveSessions.afterSession: Answer the three Think and talk questions in your book.

liveSessions.title: Grammar workshop: actions happening now
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read Grammar 1 and try its five-sentence practice on your own.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min am, is and are chant; 10 min sorting helper words with mime; 15 min the Activity 4 picture report; 10 min writing five action sentences; 5 min sharing
liveSessions.afterSession: Complete Grammar 4 and draft your five report sentences.

liveSessions.title: Non-fiction focus: plants, air and us

liveSessions.afterSession: Finish Writing 4 and check that each subheading has two sentences under it.

liveSessions.title: Guided writing: thank-you letters and pointing words

liveSessions.agenda: 5 min thank-you phrase round; 10 min appreciate without for; 20 min guided writing of the letter to the Earth; 10 min partner proofreading
liveSessions.afterSession: Finish Writing 2 and check every capital letter and full stop.

liveSessions.title: The World Around Us: promise showcase and review
liveSessions.beforeSession: Practise reading aloud the promise poster that you made in Writing 6.

liveSessions.afterSession: Complete the ten-question checkpoint and the self-assessment.

teacherNotes.noteType: AI tutor use and device safety
teacherNotes.note: Every AI tutor task in this unit assumes an adult is present. Never leave a child alone with the device: sit beside them, keep the screen visible, and close the session together. The prompts below are drawn from the source pack and grouped by file. Vocabulary: “What are some ways children can help the environment?”, “Why is picking up litter important?”, “What does each part of a plant do?”, “Why are plants important for clean air?”, “Can you help me learn more nature words?”, “Why is clean water so important?” Grammar: “Can you check my grammar answers?”, “When do I use am, is and are?”, “Can you give me five more sentences to practise this, these, that and those?” Reading and story: “Can you read the Earth poem with me and explain it?”, “Can you read the story with me and explain any hard words?”, “Can you ask me questions about the story to see if I understood?” Writing: “Can you check if my sentences are correct?”, “Can you help me make my thank-you letter to the Earth better?”

This unit sends children outdoors, so check the space first: gloves for litter tasks, no glass and no sharp items, and hand washing afterwards. Children who cannot go outside can use a window box, a potted plant or the labelled diagram instead, and every pointing task works just as well from a chair. When marking, treat a missing helping word (am, is or are) as the next step rather than as a mistake, and praise the child who chooses the right -ing spelling. Watch for the appreciate for slip in Grammar 2; correct it warmly, because it is the most common error in this unit. The story carries the unit's value: little things can make a big difference.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: 1. Planting, 2. Watering, 3. Picking up, 4. Recycling, 5. Using. Each answer must be written with a capital letter because it opens the sentence.
roots → b (take in water from the soil), stem → a (hold up the plant and carry water up), leaves → d (make food using sunlight), flower → c (make seeds for new plants).
1. True. 2. False, roots grow below the ground. 3. True. 4. False, plants need water or they will die. The two false items must be rewritten correctly.
1. am, 2. is, 3. are, 4. are, 5. is. Item 3 needs are because the children is more than one person.

Answers are the child's own ideas about nature. Check the phrase itself: glad that and happy that need a whole idea after them, appreciate takes the thing with no for, and thankful for always keeps its for.
1. This, 2. Those, 3. These, 4. That. Item 3 is plural and near, and item 4 is singular and far.
It is a special day when people all over the world show that they care about our planet. Accept any wording that includes both the caring and the whole world.
Any three of: plant trees and flowers, pick up litter, learn about recycling. All three must be different actions.
Trees and plants help clean the air we breathe. Accept clean air on its own only if the child names trees or plants as the cause.
It keeps the Earth clean and safe for animals. Both halves are needed for the mark.
No. Every day can be Earth Day if we remember to care for our planet. Reject a bare no with no reason.
Their green leaves clean the dirty air. Accept they clean the air we breathe.
They take in carbon dioxide and give out oxygen. Both gases must be in the right places.
Because they help the Earth breathe, just as our lungs help us breathe. Accept an answer that compares forests to lungs.
Plants give out the oxygen we need and clean the air in our homes and schools, so caring for them keeps us well. Look for the link between plants and our own breathing.
He is planting trees, and his uncle is helping him. Both the action and the helper are needed.
He is making paper flowers, and he is using old newspapers. Accept newspapers or old paper.
They promise to do something good for the Earth every day, not only on Mother Earth Day. The every day part carries the mark.
Blue for the sky and green for the Earth. Both pairs must be matched correctly.
A lot of nice fresh air. Accept fresh air.
Litter, which the story describes as old bags and papers lying in the soil. Accept rubbish for litter.
They put it in a box for recycling, and recycling means that fewer trees are cut down. Both parts are needed.
People had cut the trees down for wood, so the sun dried the earth and the river grew small. Award the mark for the cause and one effect.
A handful of small seeds, and she said that little things can make a big difference. Accept the words seeds and little things.
Roots, stem, leaves, flower, in that order. Award the mark for all four parts in the right order.
Trees grew along the river bank, birds began to visit again, and the air smelled fresh and clean. Two of the three changes earn the mark.
They planted and watered a few small seeds, and those seeds became a row of trees that brought the birds back and cleaned the air. Look for one small action and one large result.
Personal answer. Award the mark when the child names one real, safe action and says when they will do it.
Personal answer worth two marks: one for each feeling with its reason. Typical answers are glad or proud for a clean place, and sad or worried about litter that can hurt animals.
litter. Sunshine, oxygen and a branch are not rubbish, so only one option fits the meaning.
It turns used things into something new. Watering, counting and measuring are different jobs altogether.
The green leaves. Only leaves make food, and they use sunlight to do it.
seeds. The sentence says they grew into trees, and only seeds can do that.
These seeds in my hand are small. Near plus more than one always gives these.
Five action sentences, each with am, is or are and an -ing verb, and five reason sentences that each name one real benefit such as clean air or homes for birds.
Five report sentences: one with I am, two with he is or she is, and two with they are. Every verb must carry the -ing ending, and every sentence must describe the planting day.
A four-line card that uses I'm glad that, I'm happy that, I appreciate and I'm thankful for once each, with a different nature idea in every line. Reject any line that puts for after appreciate.
Eight tour sentences: two with this, two with these, two with that and two with those. Check that near things take this or these and that plural things take these or those.
Listen for four named things, each pointed at, with this, these, that and those used at least once each, and one fact given for every thing named.
The recording must open with Today we are and an -ing action, name who is helping, give one because reason, and close with a promise for tomorrow.
One full round in which every speaker uses I'm thankful for, each names a different thing in nature, and each child repeats the sentence of the person before them.
Three subheadings with two sentences under each. The take-in pair must name carbon dioxide and water, the give-out pair must name oxygen, and the last pair must link clean air to our own health.
Six numbered steps from Monday to Saturday. Each step names one Earth-care action from the word list and says who is helping. Repeating the same action twice loses the mark for that step.
A titled poster with three different promises beginning I promise to, one appreciate line with nothing between appreciate and the thing, and one drawing for each promise.
1. planting, 2. counting, 3. picking, 4. making, 5. recycling. Items 4 and 5 drop the silent e before -ing.

Cares for the Earth: planting a tree, watering a young plant, recycling old paper, picking up litter in the park. Harms the Earth: dropping a bottle on the path, leaving the tap running all day, burning rubbish beside the road, cutting down trees for wood.
Four sentences, one for each pointing word, written outdoors. Award the mark when near things take this or these, far things take that or those, and the plural forms name more than one thing.
Picking up litter in the park. Dropping, leaving the tap running and burning rubbish all leave harm behind.
New seeds. Soil, rain and sunlight come from around the plant, not from its flower.
Oxygen. Carbon dioxide is the gas that plants take in, and smoke and steam are not in the text at all.
is watering. Nora is one person, so are and am are both wrong, and watering on its own has no helping word.
I appreciate the tall trees. The word appreciate never takes for, to or an extra helping word.

selfAssessment.statement: I can name ways to care for the environment.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can name the parts of a plant and say what each one does.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can explain why plants and trees help clean the air.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say what someone is doing now, using am, is or are with an -ing word.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
